title: Graphs of square and cube root functions
description: Given the formula of a square-root or a cube-root function, find the appropriate graph.
topic_path: Graphs of square and cube root functions
Graphs of square and cube root functions

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The 
graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}-5$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the left $3$ units.

![The graph of two square root functions. One is the graph of the square root of x. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3). The other is the graph of the square root of x shifted to the left 3 units. It starts at (negative 3, 0) and increases through the points (negative 2, 1), (1, 2), and (6, 3). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c9124f621473c33e7eb4bd7c2bc7523717b72685)

Multiplying $\sqrt{x+3}$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x+3}$ over the $x$-axis.

![A square root function graph and its vertically reflected graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, two). The vertically reflected graph has an endpoint (negative three, zero) and passes through (one, negative two).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b582df7f8374048661d52543b3ed38ed1df2778)

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-\sqrt{x+3}$ down $5$ units.

![A square root function graph and its shifted graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has an endpoint (negative 3, zero) and passes through (one, negative two). The shifted graph has an endpoint (negative three, negative five) and passes through (one, negative seven).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/acb9d6efad3d717a391da666a7c1c8c4f2810257)

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7746e7dec8e560e8a4924a211dca36e662d1e0cd)

choices.content: ![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7746e7dec8e560e8a4924a211dca36e662d1e0cd)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the right, five units down and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/52df8daa3a33f1663c40d363b7dac11021bb84b4)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the left, five units down and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8376c37dfb7271343e3e4f937abb071f4d0e971)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted three units to the right, 5 units down and vertically reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e200eb43b9ac128ef3e1dee30c52cc19538384da)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=3\sqrt{2-x}$ as $y=3\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-2$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $2$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-2$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-2}$ over the line $x=2$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{-(x-2)}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt{-(x-2)} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  



[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(-2,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(-2, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted two units to the right and horizontally reflected.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1803926838e2717ddd70577cb7fcf28da9de4a9e)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted two units to the right, horizontally reflected, and stretched vertically by a scale factor of three.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9c3dec5a8719a7d7ea5669d5dbfe7d8e0b71735e)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted two units to the left, horizontally reflected, and stretched vertically by a scale factor of two.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ba82cf0205c04310ba5a4e61a5b640eb8f6a07c5)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It is shifted two units to the left, horizontally reflected, and stretched vertically by a scale factor of four.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09c96a5e3e0a9294faa69fc16ebb642a1913bd2e)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-\sqrt{5-x}+6$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-\sqrt{5-x}+6$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=-\sqrt{5-x}+6$ as $y=-\sqrt{-(x-5)}+6$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-5$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $5$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-5$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-5}$ over the line $x=5$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{-(x-5)}$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{-(x-5)}$ over the $x$-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $6$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-\sqrt{-(x-5)}$ up $6$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted left five units, up six units, reflected horizontally, and reflected vertically.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/57bb73fc6f308b771454e59d497ee8c1ea3a3daa)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right five units, up six units, reflected horizontally, and reflected vertically.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3d3800343a4900f2884839215624f8d3ef8a4b4d)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted left five units, up six units, and reflected vertically.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c60a54ffa52e980e91d6e163574d6efe5da10d05)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right five units, up six units, and reflected vertically.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d4ba1a1df3b6e5fc4781c68adb8eda6654a94e9e)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-1$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $1$ unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{x-1}$ by $-3$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x-1}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $3$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(5,2)$ *before* the transformation, and $(5, -6 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-3$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-3\sqrt{x-1}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $C$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right one unit, down two units, reflected horizontally, reflected vertically, and stretched by a factor of three.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1654af56ca970dacf96e4fdc7a20644104ce4042)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted left one unit, up two units, and reflected horizontally.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1853a0674cf7838e052239add6a826912236a2bb)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right one unit, up two units, reflected vertically, and stretched by a factor of three.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/80cf3885fd940ec56ac5b81bd0bdd1f981ea045c)

![A square root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right one unit, up two units, and reflected vertically.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8dbc7bd83cd63c6eba0d2c86a74ffc2f9212efa7)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ is shown below.

![The graph of the square root of x on the x y plane. It starts at (0, 0) and increases through the points (1, 1), (4, 2), and (9, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4d447646a783c7fc85a27e5d31d21541a7be7d7)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=4\sqrt{x-2}-6$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=4\sqrt{x-2}-6$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-2$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the right $2$ units.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ed86125d13571db5276bc84fbcbd66e4839c3b1e)

Multiplying $\sqrt{x-2}$ by $4$ stretches the graph of  $y=\sqrt{x-2}$ vertically by a factor of $4$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(6,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(6, 8)$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $4$.

Adding $-6$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=4\sqrt{x-2}$ down $6$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=2\sqrt{-x-1}+3$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=2\sqrt{-x-1}+3$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=2\sqrt{-x-1}+3$ as $y=2\sqrt{-(x+1)}+3$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$.

Replacing $x$ with $x+1$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt{x}$ to the left $1$ unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x+1$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt{x+1}$ over the line $x=-1$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt{-(x+1)}$ by $2$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt{-(x+1)} $ vertically by a factor of $2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

 

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(-5,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(-5, 4 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $2$.

Adding $3$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=2\sqrt{-(x+1)}$ up $3$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $D$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=2\sqrt[3]{x-4}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=2\sqrt[3]{x-4}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-4$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $4$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x-4}$ by $2$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-4} $ vertically by a factor of $2$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(-4,-2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(-4, -4 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $2$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=2\sqrt[3]{-x-4}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=2\sqrt[3]{-x-4}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=2\sqrt[3]{-x-4}$ as $y=2\sqrt[3]{-(x+4)}$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x+4$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ left $4$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x+4$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x+4}$ over the line $x=-4$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{-x-4}$ by $2$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{-x-4} $ vertically by a factor of $2$.  


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(4,-2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(4, -4 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $2$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 2]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**


[[☃ image 1]]

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted left four units and reflected horizontally.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ed684d999d04d25b10b01b6c540b08ba8bcae4c6)

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right four units, reflected horizontally, and stretched vertically by a scaled factor of two.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3ccfac297e14e1421e2bb897664dbbdc2997c24)
![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted right four units and reflected horizontally.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9b7d2d220f4bce265e6b3b7be10f7f57e42b9846)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that graph of the function passes through $(8,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(8, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $D$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted up two units.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fd6a7757881cc6b5e7a4839aec2b725bb6aec05c)

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has been shifted up two units and stretched vertically by a scaled factor of three.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f92c1ab6a2d2f702edb1bed8e9c57bebeb5777bd)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-7}-3$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-7}-3$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-7$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $7$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $-3$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-7}$ down $3$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $A$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=3\sqrt[3]{x+6}$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x+6$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ left $6$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x+6}$ by $3$ stretches the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x+6} $ vertically by a factor of $3$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(2,2)$ *before* the stretch, and $(2, 6 )$ *after* the stretch.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $3$.  

Additionally it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}+2$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $x-3$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $3$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ by $-2$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that the graph of the function passes through $(2,-1)$ *before* the transformation, and $(2, 2 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-2$.

Adding $2$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-2\sqrt[3]{x-3}$ up $2$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $C$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=-3\sqrt[3]{-x}-5$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=-3\sqrt[3]{-x}-5$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Replacing $x$ with $-x$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ over the $y$-axis.


[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $\sqrt[3]{-x}$ by $-3$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{-x}$ across the $x$-axis and stretches it vertically by a factor of $3$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that graph of the function passes through $(8,-2)$ *before* the transformation, and $(8, 6 )$ *after* the transformation.  This is because each $y$-coordinate is multiplied by $-3$.

Adding $-5$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=-3\sqrt[3]{-x}$ down $5$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $D$:**

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ is shown below.

![A cube root function graph on an x y coordinate plane. It has a middle point at (zero, zero) and passes through (negative eight, negative two) and (eight, two). ](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afeb9a9d7e4c7c5089f71d0624a6146c529ef093)

**Which of the following is the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ can be transformed
to get the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$. 
[[☃ explanation 1]]

Let's write $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}+4$ as $y=\sqrt[3]{-(x-5)}+4$ so that it is easier to see the transformations from the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$.  
Replacing $x$ with $x-5$ shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x}$ right $5$ units.

[[☃ image 1]]

Multiplying $x-5$ by $-1$ reflects the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{x-5}$ over the line $x=5$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Adding $4$ to the function shifts the graph of $y=\sqrt[3]{5-x}$ up $4$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Note that it is important to follow order of operations when building the function, as function transformations are not always commutative.  [[☃ explanation 1]]
**The graph that corresponds with these transformations is graph $B$:**


[[☃ image 1]]
